main_ingredients: whole fish (typically carp or grass carp)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet and sour fish is approximately 300 g, representing a shared main course portion for one to two diners.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and sour fish (糖醋鱼) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked fish and sweet-and-sour sauce component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for sweet and sour fish as a composite dish. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked carp/freshwater fish (NDB 15016, 15236) with sweet-and-sour sauce component data (sugar, vinegar, starch, oil). Proportions based on standard Chinese culinary recipe ratios: approximately 60% fish flesh, 15% batter/starch coating, 20% sweet-and-sour sauce, 5% aromatics. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for analogous Chinese-style battered fish dishes. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; restaurant preparation will vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fish dishes with starch coating and sweet sauce — analogous estimate based on battered/coated fish and sweet-and-sour sauce GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour fish. GI estimated at 52 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for analogous mixed dishes: battered fish (GI ~45–50) combined with sweet-and-sour sauce containing sugar and starch (GI ~60–65). Weighted composite estimate yields approximately GI 52, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (52 × 13.2 × 3) / 100 = 20.6 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Sweet and sour fish contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1140 mg per 300 g serving), representing nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and starch batter contributing to glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The sweet-and-sour sauce contains significant added sugar (approximately 7.5 g per 100 g) and the starch batter raises the glycemic load to approximately 20.6 g per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and consider requesting reduced-sugar sauce preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content with favourable omega-3 fatty acid profile from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish component provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; however, deep-frying adds oil and the dish contains moderate sodium, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and prefer steamed or lightly sauced preparations when available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Fish (糖醋鱼, Táng Cù Yú) is a classic Chinese dish in which a whole fish or fish fillets — most commonly carp, grass carp, or a similar freshwater fish — are coated in a light starch batter, deep-fried until golden and crisp, then glazed with a vibrant sweet-and-sour sauce made from sugar, vinegar, tomato paste, soy sauce, and aromatics such as garlic and ginger. The Beijiang (北疆) or Xinjiang style, sometimes called 'grape fish' (葡萄鱼) due to the decorative scoring of the fish flesh that causes it to open like a bunch of grapes when fried, is a regional variation from northern China associated with the cuisine of Xinjiang and the broader northern Chinese culinary tradition. The dish delivers a striking contrast of textures — a crispy exterior giving way to tender, flaky fish — and a bold flavour profile balancing sweetness and acidity. Nutritionally, sweet and sour fish provides moderate protein (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g) from the fish, moderate carbohydrate (approximately 13.2 g per 100 g) from the batter and sauce, and moderate fat (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying. The fish contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, B vitamins including B12, and selenium. The primary clinical considerations are the added sugar in the sauce (relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management) and the sodium from soy sauce (relevant for hypertension). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 20.6 g due to portion size. This dish is commonly served as a shared main course in Chinese restaurants in Singapore, including at Northern Chinese specialty restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for sweet and sour fish as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked freshwater fish and sweet-and-sour sauce ingredients using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar battered fish and sweet sauce dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood' — the dish is a fish-based main course, not a grain or staple. 2. Food name corrected from 'Beijiang Sweet and Sour Fish新疆葡萄鱼' to canonical English 'Sweet and Sour Fish'; the Beijiang/Xinjiang variant name and Chinese characters are preserved in aliases. 3. Cuisine tag 'Noodle' from input is not applicable to this dish and has been removed. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; the fish and vegetable oil components do not contribute trans fats at detectable levels. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1), potassium_mg (210), calcium_mg (28), iron_mg (0.8), cholesterol_mg (48), vitamin_c_mg (2.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (12), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.07), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.18), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.9), folate_mcg_dfe (8.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.6), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (145), magnesium_mg (22), zinc_mg (0.7), selenium_mcg (18), water_g (62) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked freshwater fish and are reasonable central estimates. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use. 7. Halal status marked true as the dish contains fish and plant-based ingredients with no pork or alcohol in standard preparation; restaurant-specific verification is advised. 8. Price data (SGD 32.78) from field survey input preserved in source context but not included in nutrient record as it is not a nutritional field.
